summary: Demis Hassabis discusses the path to AGI, emphasizing the need to combine large language models with AlphaZero-style planning and search. He explores the role of neuroscience in inspiring AI, the empirical 'artform' of scaling models like Gemini, and the critical importance of global governance and safety sandboxing as we approach superhuman intelligence within the next decade.

principles.statement: Push both scaling and algorithmic innovation as hard as possible.
principles.rationale: Scaling is an empirical question and might eventually hit a wall, while new algorithmic inventions are necessary but only truly valuable if they can scale.
principles.source_quote: You can think of half our effort as having to do with scaling and half our efforts having to do with inventing the next architectures and the next algorithms... my betting right now, but it's a loose betting, is that you need both. I think you've got to push both of them as hard as possible

principles.statement: Use existing human knowledge to bootstrap AGI rather than starting purely from scratch.
principles.rationale: While pure reinforcement learning from scratch is theoretically possible, leveraging the massive amounts of data and knowledge already collected on the Web is the fastest and most plausible path to AGI.
principles.source_quote: I think by far the quickest way to get to AGI, and the most plausible way, is to use all the knowledge that's existing in the world right now that we've collected from things like the Web.

principles.statement: Neuroscience provides directional inspiration for AI, not a strict blueprint.
principles.rationale: The brain serves as an existence proof that general intelligence is possible, offering high-level ideas for architectures and algorithms (like attention or experience replay), but it does not require a one-to-one mapping since AI is an engineered system.
principles.source_quote: It's not so much about a one-to-one mapping of a specific algorithm, but more so inspirational direction. Maybe it's some ideas for architecture, or algorithmic ideas, or representational ideas. The brain is an existence proof that general intelligence is possible at all.

principles.statement: The governance of superhuman AI must be a global, multi-stakeholder collaboration.
principles.rationale: The technology is too consequential and transformative to be controlled by any single company or industry; it requires international consensus to ensure it benefits all of society.
principles.source_quote: I think this is so consequential, this technology. I think it's much bigger than any one company or even industry in general. I think it has to be a big collaboration with many stakeholders from civil society, academia, government, etc.

frameworks.name: Planning atop LLMs
frameworks.when_to_apply: When building systems that require concrete goal achievement and complex reasoning beyond current LLM capabilities.
steps: Train large models to be highly accurate predictors and reliable world models.
Implement AlphaZero-like planning mechanisms on top of the world model.
Use search to explore massive spaces of possibility by chaining lines of reasoning together to achieve specific goals.
frameworks.source_quote: On top of that, we're working on things like AlphaZero-like planning mechanisms on top that make use of that model in order to make concrete plans to achieve certain goals in the world. Perhaps chaining thought, lines of reasoning, together and using search to explore massive spaces of possibility.

mental_models.name: Virtual Brain Analytics
mental_models.description: Treating the mechanistic analysis of artificial neural networks like computational neuroscience. It involves using techniques analogous to fMRI or single-cell recording to understand the internal representations and concepts built by AI systems.
mental_models.example: Applying computational neuroscience know-how to large models to understand how they build concepts, similar to the mechanistic interpretability work done by researchers like Chris Olah.

mental_models.name: Better World Models Equal Less Search
mental_models.description: The more accurate and rich a system's internal model of the world is, the less brute-force search it needs to make world-class decisions.
mental_models.example: AlphaZero looks at tens of thousands of positions compared to Deep Blue's millions, and human grandmasters look at only a few hundred, because humans and AlphaZero have much richer, more accurate models of the game.

mental_models.name: Imagination as Reconstructive Memory
mental_models.description: Imagination is not a fundamentally new process, but rather the act of pulling together familiar semantic components from memory and reassembling them in novel ways for purposes like planning and mental simulation.
mental_models.example: Using the same semantic components of human memory, but putting them together in a way the brain thinks is novel to simulate something new that helps with planning.

heuristics: Plan for success: anticipate the transformative consequences of your technology decades in advance.
Scaling is an artform: adjust the recipe and hyperparameters at each new scale rather than just repeating them.
One order of magnitude is probably about the maximum scale jump you want to do between each era of training.
A new innovation is only useful if it can also scale.

signature_quotes.text: The brain is an existence proof that general intelligence is possible at all.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing how neuroscience provides directional inspiration for AI research.

signature_quotes.text: I look at the large models today and I think they're almost unreasonably effective for what they are.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the surprising emergent properties of current LLMs.

signature_quotes.text: Scaling laws also don't just work by magic... You have to adjust the recipe and that's a bit of an art form.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the practical challenges and nuances of scaling up models like Gemini.

signature_quotes.text: A new innovation, a new invention, is only useful if it can also scale.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why significant compute is required not just for scaling, but for testing new algorithmic ideas.

signature_quotes.text: We planned for success. In 2010, no one was thinking about AI let alone AGI. But we already knew that if we could make progress... the technology created would be unbelievably transformative.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing why DeepMind started thinking about AI safety and alignment over a decade ago.

anti_patterns.description: Extrapolating scaling laws blindly across several orders of magnitude without intermediate testing.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Downstream capabilities are not always linear; there can be step functions or broken predictions, requiring intermediate data points to correct hyperparameter optimization.

anti_patterns.description: Relying purely on brute-force search without building a good world model.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is highly inefficient. Systems with better world models can make better decisions with orders of magnitude less search.

anti_patterns.description: Building AI using logic-based expert systems with massive amounts of hand-coded human priors.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The handcrafted human information often turns out to be wrong or too rigid, preventing the system from scaling and generalizing effectively.
